briefing.opening: Good day, everyone. I will now begin the climate change expert spokesperson briefing for June 10, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Accelerated Global Warming and the Crisis of the 1.5-Degree Target
sections.content: Climate-related news collected today warns that the Earth's heat accumulation rate is rapidly accelerating, putting the 1.5-degree warming defense line of the Paris Agreement at risk of collapse [1, 13]. In 2025, global warming reached 1.37°C, and research results have also been released indicating that a record-high 'energy imbalance' is accelerating global warming [45, 51, 73]. At the current pace, the 'carbon budget' for achieving the 1.5-degree target is projected to be exhausted within three years, and the Earth has once again experienced its hottest May on record or a situation close to it [4, 9, 15]. These phenomena of sea-level rise and heat accumulation are increasing the likelihood of extreme floods worldwide [14].

sections.title: ⚠️ Worsening Extreme Weather Events and Ecosystem Disruption
sections.content: The acceleration of global warming is leading to unprecedented extreme weather events and ecosystem disruption. Mega-floods that once occurred once every 100 years are now expected to increase to once every 8 years, and research has shown that climate change has also increased the likelihood of coastal floods, which were once rare [12, 37, 52, 62, 63]. In Antarctica, an unusual phenomenon of snow melting and rain falling on glaciers due to record high winter temperatures has been observed [67], and in Indonesia, heavy rainfall led to the tragic death of 7% of the rare Tapanuli orangutans [68]. Domestically, in Korea's East Sea, schools of bluefin tuna have appeared due to climate change, altering the fish species map, and Pakistan's precious trout are also threatened, indicating changes across the entire ecosystem [3, 64]. Furthermore, the complex crisis facing human society is deepening, with concerns raised about stunted growth in African children and the potential for billions of dollars in economic losses due to climate change [50, 57].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Response Efforts and Carbon Neutrality Practices
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, various efforts to respond to climate change are continuing domestically. The first marine science base has been established in the East Sea, strengthening response capabilities by observing marine ecosystems and climate change in real-time [2, 5, 6]. KTC has embarked on key technological projects for greenhouse gas and water disaster prevention [11], and President Lee Jae-myung discussed strengthening cooperation on carbon neutrality and energy transition during a summit with the EU [16, 26, 30]. Local governments and educational institutions are also actively engaged in campaigns, environmental education, talent development, and carbon neutrality point incentive programs to promote carbon neutrality practices [17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 28, 25, 29]. Furthermore, Nambu Power has simultaneously achieved carbon neutrality and regional co-prosperity by cultivating smart farm tomatoes using waste heat, and specific practical examples, such as the full-scale promotion of the Carbon Neutral Green City project in Chungju, are drawing attention [23, 27].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Fund Creation and Renewable Energy Expansion
sections.content: The international community is also accelerating cooperation and the transition to renewable energy to address climate change. At the 8th Global Environment Facility (GEF) Assembly, 186 countries agreed to establish an environmental fund worth 3.4 billion Euros, laying a financial foundation for climate action [10]. Globally, the construction of renewable energy sources such as solar and offshore wind power is actively underway, and positive news has also been reported that offshore wind power in South Australia is expected to remain strong despite climate change [34, 65]. Companies like Henkel are accelerating decarbonization by establishing carbon-neutral manufacturing facilities in India and Türkiye [66]. Furthermore, successful examples of adaptation efforts have been reported, such as global rice production nearly doubling thanks to human management despite climate change [39]. Experts emphasize that responding to climate change is not too expensive, and discussions on building user-driven climate adaptation products are also ongoing [48, 58].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of global warming, along with various response efforts underway both domestically and internationally. Global solidarity and proactive action to overcome the climate crisis are more crucial than ever. This concludes the climate briefing for June 10, 2026.

articles.title: From Nuisance to Power Generation Fuel…The Remarkable Transformation of 'Cow Manure'
articles.original_title: 골칫거리에서 발전 연료로…‘소똥’의 멋진 변신
articles.description: Visiting Jeonbuk Gimje Resource Circulation Center, the nation's first 'cow manure solid fuel production' facility. Choi In-kyu, head of the cow manure fuelization promotion team at Jeonbuk Gimje Resource Circulation Center, points to the high-speed fermentation and drying facility for cow manure solid fuel. Over 50% cow manure is mixed with sawdust and other materials, then fermented and dried, and blended with coal at a 94:6 ratio. This initiative aims to resolve livestock manure disposal difficulties, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and offers significant economic benefits. The province plans to invest 198.8 billion won by 2029 to establish production facilities across five cities and counties.

summary.phenomenon: The nation's first 'cow manure solid fuel production' facility at Jeonbuk Gimje Resource Circulation Center was visited. Choi In-kyu, head of the cow manure fuelization promotion team, pointed to the high-speed fermentation and drying facility for cow manure solid fuel. Over 50% cow manure is mixed with sawdust and other materials, then fermented and dried, and blended with coal at a 94:6 ratio, solving livestock manure disposal problems and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

articles.original_description: 전국 첫 ‘우분 고체연료 생산’ 전북 김제자원순환센터 가보니최인규 전북 김제자원순환센터 우분연료화 추진단장이 우분 고체연료화 고속발효·건조시설을 가리키고 있다.소똥 50% 이상에 톱밥 등 섞어 발효·건조…석탄과 ‘94 대 6’ 혼합 축산분뇨 처리난 해결하고 온실가스 감축, 경제성도 큰 기대 모아 도, 2029년까지 1988억원 투입해 5개 시군에 생산시설 ···

articles.title: Record winter temperatures in Antarctic raise fears over speed of climate breakdown
articles.description: Temperatures above 15C ‘very strange’ say scientists, as snow melts and rain falls on glaciers in usually frozen regionTemperatures in the Antarctic climbed above 15C this month, shattering the previous winter heat record for the usually frozen region and raising concerns about the speed of climate breakdown.The new winter peak temperature was logged by the Argentinian Esperanza base on the Trinity peninsula on 6 June amid a protracted heatwave, when the maximum daily temperature exceeded zero d

summary.phenomenon: On June 6, temperatures at Argentina's Esperanza base on the Trinity Peninsula in Antarctica exceeded 15C, breaking the region's previous winter heat record. Unusually, melting snow and rain falling on glaciers were observed.
summary.outlook: Scientists stated that these phenomena are raising concerns about the speed of climate breakdown.

articles.original_description: Temperatures above 15C ‘very strange’ say scientists, as snow melts and rain falls on glaciers in usually frozen regionTemperatures in the Antarctic climbed above 15C this month, shattering the previous winter heat record for the usually frozen region and raising concerns about the speed of climate breakdown.The new winter peak temperature was logged by the Argentinian Esperanza base on the Trinity peninsula on 6 June amid a protracted heatwave, when the maximum daily temperature exceeded zero d

articles.title: 인도네시아 나흘간의 집중호우로 세계 희귀 유인원 7% 사망, 연구 결과 밝혀져
articles.original_title: Four days of extreme rain in Indonesia killed 7% of world’s rarest great apes, study finds
articles.description: Critically endangered Tapanuli orangutan population falls after heavy rain and landslides, fuelled by climate crisis, in North SumatraExtreme rainfall and landslides fuelled by the climate crisis killed 7% of the remaining population of the world’s rarest great ape, a study has found, prompting fears for the species’ survival.The research suggests 58 out of the remaining 800 critically endangered Tapanuli orangutans (Pongo tapanuliensis) were killed after more than 1,000mm (39in) of rain fell ov

summary.phenomenon: In North Sumatra, Indonesia, four days of extreme rainfall and landslides, fueled by the climate crisis, resulted in the deaths of 58 (7%) of the 800 remaining Tapanuli orangutans, the world's rarest great ape. More than 1,000mm of rain fell during this period.
summary.cause: The climate crisis fueled the extreme rainfall and landslides, leading to a decline in the Tapanuli orangutan population.
summary.outlook: This research raises concerns about the survival of the species.

articles.original_description: Critically endangered Tapanuli orangutan population falls after heavy rain and landslides, fuelled by climate crisis, in North SumatraExtreme rainfall and landslides fuelled by the climate crisis killed 7% of the remaining population of the world’s rarest great ape, a study has found, prompting fears for the species’ survival.The research suggests 58 out of the remaining 800 critically endangered Tapanuli orangutans (Pongo tapanuliensis) were killed after more than 1,000mm (39in) of rain fell ov

articles.original_title: Trump targeting immigrants from countries hit most by climate shocks
articles.description: A Guardian analysis reveals how most of 39 countries facing US entry restrictions are most vulnerable environmentally‘Every day it’s more barriers’: how the US is shutting out climate refugeesDonald Trump’s immigration crackdown is largely targeting people from the countries most vulnerable to displacement from climate-driven disasters, a Guardian analysis shows.As the Trump administration pushes policies to boost planet-heating fossil fuels, millions of people are being forced to flee their hom

summary.phenomenon: According to a Guardian analysis, the Donald Trump administration's immigration crackdown primarily targets individuals from countries most vulnerable to displacement due to climate change-driven disasters, among the 39 nations facing US entry restrictions.
summary.cause: While the Trump administration pursued policies that boost planet-heating fossil fuels, millions of people were forced to flee their homes due to climate change-induced disasters.

summary.phenomenon: Climate scientists have revised downward the worst-case scenario for high-emissions.
summary.outlook: The best-case scenario has now become implausible.

articles.title: ‘Every day it’s more barriers’: how the US is shutting out climate refugees
articles.description: As the US shuts its doors to most refugees, there’s little hope of a new system to help those forced from home by climate impactsTrump targets migrants from countries hit most by climate shocksMillions of people around the world are having their lives upended by floods, storms and heatwaves worsened by the climate crisis. Those forced to flee their home countries, however, are finding that the door to the US is more firmly shut than ever.Neither US nor international law recognizes environmental

summary.phenomenon: Millions of lives worldwide have been disrupted, forcing people to leave their home countries due to floods, storms, and heatwaves exacerbated by the climate crisis. However, the United States is increasingly closing its doors to these climate refugees.
summary.cause: The climate crisis intensifies extreme weather events, leading to population displacement. Furthermore, neither US nor international law recognizes environmental refugees, creating barriers for them to find refuge.
summary.outlook: With the US doors more firmly shut than ever, there is little hope for a new system to help those displaced from their homes by climate impacts.

articles.title: The rightwing campaign to control how US judges view the climate crisis
articles.description: US energy secretary Chris Wright featured in seminars to judges when he was a fracking executiveAs cities and states sue big oil for billions in damages over allegations that it covered up the dangers of its products, rightwing organizations are attempting to discredit the wave of litigation. They claim the lawyers behind it are teaming up with an environmentally focused legal education non-profit to bias federal judges against oil companies.But it is actually fossil fuel-backed organizations th

summary.phenomenon: Cities and states across the US are filing multi-billion dollar lawsuits against major oil companies, alleging they concealed the dangers of their products. Concurrently, right-wing organizations are attempting to discredit this wave of litigation.
summary.cause: Right-wing organizations claim that the lawyers behind the lawsuits are collaborating with an environmentally focused legal education non-profit to bias federal judges against oil companies. The article implies that fossil fuel-backed organizations are influencing judges.
